name: Good-better-best framing
logic: Present three tiers and most buyers pick the middle — and a premium 'best' anchors the range so the 'better' looks reasonable. For a manufacturer catalogue this turns a flat SKU grid into a guided decision: single-wall (good), double-wall (better), ripple-wall (best). HBR's research shows good-better-best both attracts price-sensitive buyers with the 'good' option and lifts spend from those who want more, with the middle tier the most-chosen. Pair it with a clear comparison table and you've replaced 'which do I need?' with an obvious answer.

evidence.title: The Good-Better-Best Approach to Pricing
evidence.note: Rafi Mohammed, HBR Sep–Oct 2018: a 'good' tier attracts new customers, 'best' lifts spend; the middle option is the most-selected.

evidence.title: Comparison Tables for Products, Services, and Features
evidence.note: NN/g: comparison is a critical pre-purchase activity; prebuilt tables (≤5 options) help users decide quickly.

exemplars.name: Allstate 'Your Choice' tiers
exemplars.why: HBR's cited example — value/standard/premium tiers; 3.9M policies sold by 2008.

metricsNote: Most buyers choose the middle of three tiers (HBR); choice overload falls when options are clearly differentiated.
